summary: This slice was small once constrained to checker-owned builtins plus one end-to-end schema/runtime regression; trying to retrofit every existing runtime audit object would have turned it into a larger trust-runtime refactor.
ergonomics: Checker-owned builtin record declarations make adding new schema primitives straightforward because they automatically flow into lowering and schema export surfaces.
The existing auth identity regression was a good anchor for extending into audit schemas without adding a brand new fixture.
Clasp source formatting for long constructor calls is still a little brittle; multiline application with leading parentheses failed parse expectations and had to be collapsed onto one line.
follow_ups: Standardize workflow, policy, secret, and simulation audit payloads onto the new audit schema family so runtime-produced audit records share one contract.
Add a first-class helper for deriving an audit actor from `AuthSession` or policy context to reduce manual `auditActor` construction in user code.
Consider a typed event wrapper on top of `StandardAuditEnvelope` if future tasks need envelope-plus-payload conventions across boundary logs.
warnings: Do not name a user record `StandardAuditEnvelope`, `AuditActor`, `AuditAction`, or `AuditProvenance` now that they are compiler-known schemas.
Targeted `cabal test` outside `nix develop` fails in this workspace because `ghc` is not on the host path; use the nix shell for focused verification.
The parser did not accept the initial multiline `auditEnvelope` call style used in the fixture; keep these constructor applications on one line unless parser support changes.
residual_risks: Generated workflow/policy/runtime audit entries still use ad hoc JS object shapes rather than automatically materializing the new `StandardAuditEnvelope` contract.

`StandardAuditEnvelope` avoids colliding with existing user-defined `AuditEnvelope` records, but that also means there is not yet a single canonical bare `AuditEnvelope` type name in the language surface.
